title: Rolling Shop Cart / Outfeed Table
summary: Plywood cart on locking casters, sized to double as table saw outfeed. Solves two problems with one box.
description:
Build the cart so its top sits exactly 1/8" *below* your table saw's table. Then it's not just a cart — it's an outfeed table, and you stop wrestling long boards off the back of the saw alone.

That one measurement is the entire design constraint. Everything else is a plywood box on wheels.

Locking casters, all four. Three locking and one not is how a cart pivots away from you at the worst moment.

tools.note: Or a track saw. Breaking down sheets.
Fast plywood joinery. Exactly the right tool for a shop cart.

materials.name: Birch or shop-grade plywood, 3/4", 4x8
materials.note: Shop-grade is fine here. Nobody is inspecting your cart's veneer.

materials.name: Locking swivel casters, 3"
materials.note: ALL FOUR must lock. Rated for 200+ lbs each — this cart will end up holding far more than you think.

materials.name: Clear interior finish (hardwax oil or wipe-on poly)
materials.note: Optional, but a sealed top wipes clean and lets work slide instead of catching.

cutList.note: Height depends on YOUR saw. See step 1 — do not use this number blindly.
Full plywood back. This is what stops the cart racking as it rolls over a cord.
Optional. Splits the shelves so things don't slide around.

steps.title: Measure your saw first. This dictates everything.
steps.body:
Measure from the floor to the top of your table saw's table.

**Cart height = saw height − plywood top thickness (3/4") − caster height − 1/8".**

That 1/8" is deliberate: the cart top sits just *below* the saw table, so a board sliding off the saw glides onto the cart instead of catching on its edge. A cart even 1/16" too tall will snag every single board and you'll hate it.

Adjust the side height in the cut list to whatever your arithmetic gives you.

steps.title: Break down the sheets
steps.body:
Circular saw and straight-edge to rough size, then trim to final on the table saw.

Don't try to muscle full 4x8 sheets over the table saw solo — that's how sheets get away from you.

steps.title: Assemble the box
steps.body:
Pocket-hole the shelves to the sides, then add the back. Glue and screw everything.

The back is structural — it's what keeps the cart from folding into a parallelogram when you shove it across a shop floor. Don't skip it or substitute thin ply.

steps.title: Attach the top
steps.body:
Screw the top down from underneath through the shelves, or pocket-hole from the inside. Countersink anything that comes through the top surface.

**Nothing may sit proud of the top.** A screw head 1/32" proud will gouge the underside of every board you slide across it.

steps.title: Mount the casters
steps.body:
Four locking swivel casters, one at each corner, bolted (not just screwed) through the bottom shelf.

All four lock. Two locking casters means the cart still pivots — usually right when you're pushing a heavy board through the saw.

steps.title: Check the height, then use it
steps.body:
Roll it up behind the saw. Lay a straight-edge from the saw table across the cart top.

The cart should be a hair low. If it's high, you have a problem — take it apart or shim the saw. If it's low by more than 1/4", shim the casters.

Then seal the top, load the shelves, and enjoy never chasing a falling board off the back of the saw again.
materials: Clear interior finish (hardwax oil or wipe-on poly)
